skill.name: ppt-orchestra-skill
skill.description: Plan and orchestrate multi-slide PowerPoint creation from scratch. Use before generating a full deck with subagents: classify each slide type, enforce visual variety, set typography/spacing rules, and run text-based QA to catch content issues.
skill.summary: Plan a complete PowerPoint deck, classify slide types, guide visual variety, and require content QA before delivery.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged dynamic require, shell commands, weak cryptography patterns, and reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. Review found no malicious intent, no network exfiltration, and no prompt injection; most high-risk hits are false positives from presentation text and fenced examples. The remaining risk is legitimate but meaningful because the skill instructs users to create and execute local JavaScript and install dependencies.

medium_findings.title: Generated JavaScript Execution Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to generate slide JavaScript files, dynamically require them, and run node compile.js. This is expected for PPTX generation, but generated scripts can execute arbitrary local code if a malicious slide file is introduced.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The dynamic require and node execution workflow is explicit in the documented compile process. The context is legitimate presentation generation, so the concern is execution risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Dependency Installation and External Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill tells users to run python, grep, pip install, and npm install commands. These commands support PPTX generation and QA, but they can alter the local environment and depend on package trust.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly documented and relevant to the skill purpose. No evidence shows hostile arguments or exfiltration, but package installation and command execution are real operational risks.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithm patterns across SKILL.md, but the file contains presentation guidance and code examples, not cryptographic operations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or password hashing behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines showed deck planning, layout advice, and PPTX build instructions. There are no crypto APIs or algorithm names used for security decisions.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported reconnaissance patterns, but the cited lines describe slide layouts, common design mistakes, and text extraction QA. No evidence found of hostname, user, OS, process, or network enumeration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is natural-language presentation guidance. It does not request system information or enumerate host details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Require of Generated Slide Modules
dangerous_patterns.description: The compile example loads slide files with a variable require path. This enables modular slide generation, but each required file runs as JavaScript during compilation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The variable require statement is present and executes local slide modules. It is not malicious by itself, but it is a clear code execution pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in User Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands for node execution, PPTX text extraction, grep checks, and dependency installation. Users should inspect generated files and install packages from trusted sources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are visible in the documented workflow and are appropriate for the task. Their risk depends on the local environment and package source integrity.

content.value_statement: Large slide decks often become inconsistent, repetitive, and hard to review. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for planning, generating, and checking PowerPoint decks.
actual_capabilities: Classifies every deck slide into a standard page type.
Defines typography, spacing, and layout rules for consistent slides.
Guides visual variety across covers, dividers, content pages, and closing slides.
Provides a PPTX compilation pattern using generated slide modules.
Requires text extraction QA to find missing content and placeholders.
Lists dependencies needed for PPTX creation and review.
limitations: It provides workflow instructions, not a complete slide generator by itself.
It assumes users can run local Node.js and Python commands.
It does not verify visual rendering beyond text-based QA guidance.
It uses fixed example slide counts that must be adjusted per deck.

use_cases.title: Create a Client Proposal Deck
use_cases.description: Plan a consistent proposal with a cover, section dividers, comparison slides, data slides, and a closing action page.

use_cases.title: Prepare an Internal Strategy Presentation
use_cases.description: Structure a multi-section business deck with varied layouts, clear hierarchy, and repeatable quality checks.

use_cases.title: Generate Training Materials
use_cases.description: Organize lessons into section dividers, process slides, examples, summaries, and review-ready PPTX output.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Simple Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ppt-orchestra-skill to plan a 6-slide PowerPoint deck about [topic]. Include slide type, purpose, layout, and key content for each slide.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Slide Variety
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this deck outline with ppt-orchestra-skill. Identify repeated layouts and suggest stronger visual variety without changing the message.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Build Instructions
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ppt-orchestra-skill to convert this outline into slide-by-slide build guidance for a PPTX generated with pptxgenjs.

prompt_templates.title: Run Deck Quality Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ppt-orchestra-skill to define a QA checklist for this generated PowerPoint. Include missing content, placeholder text, order, spacing, and contrast checks.

output_examples.input: Plan an investor update deck for a SaaS startup.
output: A cover slide with company name, reporting period, and confident visual tone.
A table of contents with three sections: progress, metrics, and next steps.
Content slides using data callouts, comparison layouts, and process views.
A closing slide with funding priorities and requested actions.

output_examples.input: Review a deck outline that uses bullet lists on every slide.
output: Replace repeated bullet slides with comparison columns, process steps, and visual stat callouts.
Use left-aligned body text and stronger title-to-body size contrast.
Add images, icons, charts, or shapes where they support the message.

output_examples.input: Define QA steps for a generated training presentation.
output: Extract slide text and check for missing sections, typos, and wrong order.
Search for placeholder terms such as lorem, ipsum, and layout notes.
Fix each issue, then verify the affected slides again before delivery.

best_practices: Choose a slide type for every slide before generating content.
Use consistent margins, spacing, and typography across the full deck.
Run at least one fix-and-verify cycle before final delivery.
anti_patterns: Do not use the same layout for every content slide.
Do not deliver text-only slides when visual structure is needed.
Do not skip placeholder and extracted-text checks after generation.

faq.question: Does this skill create PowerPoint files directly?
faq.answer: It provides planning, compilation, and QA instructions. A separate generation step creates the actual PPTX file.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why does it mention JavaScript?
faq.answer: The workflow uses pptxgenjs slide modules and a compile script to assemble a PowerPoint deck.

faq.question: Is it safe to run the generated compile script?
faq.answer: Inspect generated slide files first. They run as local JavaScript during compilation.

faq.question: Can it help with visual design?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives rules for slide types, layout variety, typography, spacing, contrast, and common mistakes.

faq.question: Does it check final slide visuals?
faq.answer: It focuses on text-based QA. Users should still inspect exported slides visually.
